Name: latency-aware_layer-skipping
Title: Latency-Aware Layer-Skipping via Jacobian-Based Spectral Stability Bounds
Short Hypothesis: Jacobian-based spectral stability bounds can enable training-free, latency-aware layer-skipping mechanisms that enforce strict wall-clock budgets while minimizing hidden-state perturbation.
Related Work: Existing approaches like Spectral Quantization Routing (SQR) and Training-Free Geometric Interference Cancellation focus on mixed-precision inference and geometric filtering but do not address layer-skipping directly. Our proposal leverages Jacobian-based spectral stability bounds to introduce a novel layer-skipping mechanism, distinct from these methods.
Abstract: We propose a novel training-free framework for latency-aware layer-skipping in deep neural networks, utilizing Jacobian-based spectral stability bounds. Our approach computes these bounds on-the-fly using randomized SVD, mapping them to a layer-skipping policy that enforces strict wall-clock budgets. We theoretically ground the mechanism with PAC-Bayes bounds that link spectral stability to generalization stability under perturbation. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, our method achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic layer-skipping baselines. By replacing qualitative fragility claims with formalized risk bounds, our framework provides a transparent, reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference under natural distribution shifts.
Experiments: Evaluate the layer-skipping mechanism on WILDS benchmarks using single-GPU setups.
Compare throughput-accuracy Pareto frontiers against static and heuristic layer-skipping baselines.
Measure hidden-state perturbation and stability using spectral stability bounds.
Validate theoretical PAC-Bayes bounds linking spectral stability to generalization stability under perturbation.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The randomized SVD computation might introduce additional overhead.
The layer-skipping mechanism might not generalize well to all architectures.
Strict wall-clock budgets might limit the applicability in highly dynamic environments.
